design_philosophy.core_loop: Push boxes onto designated storage locations by planning moves ahead, avoiding deadlock states where boxes become permanently trapped
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk of irreversible deadlock—careless moves can make puzzles unsolvable, requiring undo or restart
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Warehouse logistics puzzle solver; mastering spatial reasoning and consequential decision-making
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Sokoban is fundamentally opinionated about consequence: every move matters permanently unless undone. No pulls allowed (only pushes), creating asymmetric physics that punishes inefficiency. The game trusts players to understand failure is self-inflicted through poor planning, not unfair mechanics

difficulty.checkpointing: Undo system allows reverting moves; no traditional checkpoint structure
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High—players can undo mistakes, allowing self-paced learning. Difficulty emerges from puzzle design requiring multi-move planning and deadlock prevention, not execution pressure

progression.unlock_mechanism: Level-by-level progression through curated puzzle sequences; original 1982 version featured 20 levels
replayability_hooks: Puzzle editor allowing custom level creation
Optimization challenges—solving same puzzle with fewer moves

world_building_techniques: Warehouse setting provides thematic coherence for box-pushing mechanic
Visual clarity through top-down perspective and minimal aesthetic

environmental_storytelling: Warehouse layout communicates puzzle constraints visually
Box placement and wall configuration tell solution-space story

sources_used: wikipedia—Sokoban article (gameplay mechanics, history, design origins)
